TASK ACCOUNTING IN THE BUG TRACKER
At any given time, there must be at least one task assigned to you in the "In Progress" status.
If you are working on a project alone, you create tasks and keep track of them yourself.
For a project in development, this means you need to present the work plan in the form of tasks,
break down a large task into subtasks, give time estimates for those subtasks.
For a maintenance project, these are ongoing routine tasks.
Otherwise, the tasks are created by your technical manager.
Give the task a runtime estimate when you create it.
Change the status of the task when you take the task to work, send it for testing or review.
A task should have an executor!
The "Testing" and "Review" statuses are assigned to a task only if it is relevant for this project.
IN WORK -> TESTING When a task is transferred to testing, the tester is set as the executor.
TESTING -> IN WORK When a task is returned for revision, the developer is the executor.
TESTING -> DONE When a task is transferred to the "ready" status, the task is assigned to the project manager.
For testing tasks, there should always be a primary task:
- for development (something the developer has been working on and now it needs to be tested)
or a regular daily test (daily/regularly tested builds).
In the first case, the developer transfers the task to the tester, the task is closed after acceptance.
In the second case, the task can be open forever (with assignment to a tester), time and notes are logged into it.
Mark the progress of the task.
Note the time spent daily.
If you need to attach a file (log, screenshot, etc.)
, you physically attach the file to the task.
Do NOT provide links to file sharing sites.
Keep working notes in the comments of the task.
If there is a difficult problem on the way to the goal,
or you've found a solution to a problem,
or if you have a question - write a comment.
Don't forget to give links to the source of the solution.
